dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Sarah, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She's been really short of breath since last night. Looks like she's trying to breathe through an oxygen mask.
Ma'am, can you tell me who you are?
I'm Martha, I'm 69.
Where are we right now, Martha?
I'm at my apartment.
Can you tell me what the date is today?
March? Maybe 25th? I'm not exactly sure.
What happened today?
I started having trouble breathing last night. I called 911 today when my nebulizer stopped working.
Ma'am, we're going to do a quick check to make sure you can breathe well, okay?
I don't see any signs of blockage in your airway. Let me evaluate your breathing.
Ma'am, I'll need to count your breaths for me.
Your breathing is fast, about 21 breaths per minute.
Martha, I'm going to place this mask on you to help with your breathing.
I'll check your oxygen saturation now with an oximetry clip.
You're at 94%-that's a bit low, but we'll keep an eye on it.
Let me check your pulse and blood pressure.
Your pulse is very rapid-117 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is currently elevated-142 over 86.
We're going to run a heart monitor now.
Got it. The heart monitor shows a rapid, wide-complex tachycardia with signs of agonal idioventricular rhythm.
Martha, can you tell me a bit more about the breathing issues and how you're feeling?
For me, the shortness of breath started becoming noticeable yesterday. I've been relying on my nebulizer at home.
Does any medical history stand out that you'd like to tell us about?
I have a history of COPD and congestive heart failure.
Are you taking any medications regularly?
Yes-Xarelto, Lasix, Coreg, Plavix, atorvastatin.
Have you started any new medications or noticed any side effects?
No, all current meds are things I take regularly.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your breathing effort? 0 is normal breathing, 10 is the worst shortness of breath you've ever felt.
I'd say it's at an 8. I feel like I'm barely getting air.
Has the shortness of breath spread to anywhere else, like your arms or jaw?
Not really, but I get a bit lightheaded when I move too fast.
Can I look over your skin to check for any signs of distress or color changes?
Yes.
Your skin is slightly pale, but not blue. It's warm with normal turgor and no rashes or bruises.
May I examine your head and neck for any issues?
Sure.
Pupils are equal and reactive, and I'm not seeing any signs of swelling or discomfort in your neck area.
Are you experiencing any throat or mouth discomfort, like a dry sensation or soreness?
Just dry. I haven't had much fluid today.
I'm going to listen to your lungs. This may be a bit cold.
Go ahead.
I hear loud wheezing on both sides. This could be an exacerbation of your COPD.
I'm going to assist your breathing with CPAP now.
You mentioned you used it before during an episode. This should help you breathe a bit easier.
Rechecking after the new oxygen setup-how are your breathing and saturation levels now?
Still 21 breaths per minute and your saturation is up a bit to 95%. That's good progress.
I'm going to give you a nebulizer treatment with albuterol and Atrovent to help open your airways.
I'll now perform a second 12-lead ECG to get a more detailed look at your heart rhythm.
Your pulse is still rapid at 117 and blood pressure remains at 142 over 86 overall stable so far.
It's time to carefully move you onto the cot to bring you to the ambulance.
Do you have a preference for the hospital you'd like to go to, Martha?
St. Mary's is fine with me.
We're moving you now. Stay as still as you can while I help lift and support you.
We've selected St. Mary's as our destination. Everything's set for transport.
We are now in the ambulance. Your CPAP and nebulizer are both set up.
En route to St. Mary's ED. You're tolerating the treatments so far.
Martha, how is your shortness of breath feeling now with the treatments in place?
It feels a bit better. I'd say it's down to a 6.
That's a helpful improvement. Can you describe how it feels now?
It's like a firm weight on my chest. Still there, but not so overwhelming.
Let me quickly do a blood sugar check to ensure there are no additional issues.
According to the EPCR, your blood sugar was listed as 0. Critical reading, but we didn't treat it in the field.
I'm going to try to start an IV in an arm or leg vein to prepare for possible medication.
I made three attempts but cannot locate an IV site. No more attempts will be made; further IV will occur at the hospital.
You mentioned a pacemaker or an AICD before. Were you told anything recently about its status?
Yes, I had one turned off at St. Mary's some time ago.
I see you used to track your oxygen levels at home. What did the device say before we got here?
My finger monitor said I was at 93%. They took it away.
You are awake and alert, oriented to person, place, time, and events (A&O x4).
Rechecking-that's still 117 pulse and 142 over 86 blood pressure-no significant changes.
I'll relay to the ED about the rhythm and your current meds-no attempts at adenosine due to IV failure.
Also no phone number available. Not listed on your documentation or known to you.
I'll assist you from the cot into the ED bed when we arrive. Just follow my lead.
Your CPAP and oxygen support will also be transferred and set up at the ED bed.
ED has been alerted and knows your treatment history in the field.
Looking again-your pulse is 117, breaths 21, and oxygen around 95%. Still stable.
How are you doing with the breathing effort now?
Still about a 6, but it's manageable.
We're now walking into the hospital doors. Secure on the stretcher-no issues.
Securing you into the ED bed now-everything, including your CPAP, is connected and ready.
Already passed to the ED your A&OX4 mental status, pulse reading, and IV attempts.
I'll now inform the ED team about your medications and the steps we've taken in the field.
Last check-one more time in the ED. Your pulse is still 117, breaths 21, and oxygen 95%. Very stable.
You're still at a 6. That's the lowest it's been so far.
The nebulizer is keeping your airways open. Just stay still and breathe it in slowly.
Your condition has remained stable throughout. We'll continue to monitor in the ED.
Class of Transport is listed as "Hot Class 15"-highest priority for emergency care.
I'll update the ED staff with a full list of your medications for continued care.
Let me confirm that ED is aware you are coming in and your status are all accounted for.
You're doing really well, Martha. Everything's covered, and the ED team is ready for you.
How's the breathing feeling now as we enter the ED?
It's a 6. I'll let them know if it changes.
